title: Air-fryer Scotch eggs
subtitle: Crisp breadcrumb coating with a jammy or hard-boiled centre
excerpt: Hard-boiled or soft-boiled eggs wrapped in seasoned sausage meat, coated in panko breadcrumbs, and cooked in the air fryer at 180°C for 18 minutes. The lower temperature lets the heat reach the sausage-meat centre before the breadcrumb exterior over-browns.
sourceNotes: The Scotch egg is a British invention most commonly associated with Fortnum and Mason, which claims to have created it in 1738. The traditional recipe deep-fries the assembled egg; the air fryer version achieves a comparable result without the oil. The preference for a jammy rather than fully hard-boiled centre dates from the gastropub trend of the 2010s and is now the standard expectation.

content.text: Cool the boiled eggs in ice water immediately after cooking. The ice bath stops the cooking and contracts the egg white slightly, which makes peeling cleaner and prevents the green ring that appears when eggs are overcooked. A cracked yolk on peeling almost always comes from skipping this step.

items.name: Sausage meat
items.prepNote: or squeeze meat from 6 good-quality pork sausages

content.text: Bring a pan of water to a boil. Lower 4 of the {{eggs}} in and cook for 6 minutes for a jammy centre or 8 minutes for fully hard-boiled. Transfer immediately to a bowl of ice water for 5 minutes, then peel carefully. Season the {{sausage-meat}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Divide into 4 portions. Flatten each portion into a disc in the palm of your hand, place a peeled egg in the centre, and wrap the meat around it evenly, sealing the joins firmly. Refrigerate for 30 minutes. Beat the remaining 2 {{eggs}} in a shallow bowl. Set up three shallow bowls: seasoned {{plain-flour}}, beaten egg, {{breadcrumbs-panko}}. Roll each wrapped egg in flour, then egg, then press into the breadcrumbs. Preheat the air fryer to 180°C. Cook for 18 minutes, turning once at the 10-minute mark, until deep golden all over.

items.fix: Press the meat joins firmly before coating. If the meat feels stiff and crumbly when wrapping, let it sit at room temperature for 5 minutes to become pliable: do not let it warm through
items.cause: Joins not sealed properly, or meat too cold to adhere after chilling
items.symptom: Sausage meat separating from the egg during cooking

items.fix: A light dusting of flour is enough. The egg wash must cover the entire surface for the breadcrumbs to adhere. If the coating is still falling off, double-coat: flour, egg, breadcrumbs, egg again, breadcrumbs again
items.cause: Flour layer too thick, or egg wash missed spots
items.symptom: Breadcrumb coating coming off in the basket

items.fix: 180°C is deliberate: the lower temperature allows heat to reach the centre before the outside over-browns. If still pink at 18 minutes, add 3 to 4 minutes at 160°C. Press gently: the meat should feel firm, not soft
items.cause: Scotch eggs too large, or temperature too high
items.symptom: Coating browned but sausage meat pink inside

content.text: Air-fryer Scotch eggs are a pub-style snack, a picnic staple, or a component of a shareable platter. Serve with English mustard and a dressed green salad, or halved with a brown sauce dip. The jammy-centred version works best served warm within an hour of cooking.
